metadata.title: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = photon-as-particle / momentum transfer / wavelength shift; CRIMSON = classical wave / zero shift predicted / wrong model
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no relativistic kinematics algebra, no Thomson scattering cross-section, no photoelectric recap. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Shine X-rays at electrons and the scattered X-rays come back with a longer wavelength. The light lost energy to the collision — as if it were a billiard ball.

card.copy: Why X-Rays Change Color When They Bounce Off Electrons

beats.narration_text: Classical wave theory says scattered X-rays should come back at the same frequency — the wave shakes the electron and the electron radiates at the same rate. Compton's 1923 experiment showed a clear wavelength shift. The wave theory was wrong.

production_viz.label: classical: scattered frequency unchanged; Compton: shift observed

beats.narration_text: X-rays at 0.071 nanometers hit graphite and emerge at 0.073 nanometers at 90 degrees. That's 2 picometers of extra wavelength. Classical electrodynamics predicts zero shift. What is carrying away the energy?

card.copy: X-rays at 0.071 nm scatter to 0.073 nm at 90°. Classical predicts zero shift. What carries the energy?

beats.narration_text: If light carries momentum p equals h over lambda, then a photon-electron collision is like two billiard balls colliding. The photon gives up some momentum to the recoiling electron. Less momentum means longer wavelength — since p equals h over lambda, more lambda means less p.

production_viz.label: photon ball + electron ball collision: momentum transfer, wavelength grows

beats.narration_text: The wavelength shift is delta-lambda equals h over m-e-c, times 1 minus cosine-theta. At theta equals 90 degrees: delta-lambda equals h over m-e-c — the Compton wavelength — which is 2.426 picometers. Compton measured 2.4 picometers. Match to four significant figures.

production_viz.label: delta_lambda = (h/mec)(1-cos theta), 90 deg: 2.426 pm measured 2.4 pm

beats.narration_text: The recoiling electron carries away the energy the photon lost. Energy conservation: h-nu equals h-nu-prime plus the electron's kinetic energy. Momentum conservation in two directions. The photon-as-particle picture satisfies both simultaneously. The wave picture cannot.

production_viz.label: energy + momentum conservation: photon particle gives KE to electron

beats.narration_text: The Compton effect established that light has momentum, not just energy. A photon with wavelength lambda carries momentum h over lambda — exactly as de Broglie would say for a particle. Wave-particle duality is not just for energy. It is for momentum too.

production_viz.label: p = h/lambda: photon carries both energy and momentum

beats.narration_text: At theta equals 180 degrees — backscatter — delta-lambda equals 2h over m-e-c, which is 4.85 picometers: the maximum shift. At theta equals 0 degrees — forward scatter — delta-lambda equals zero. The shift is zero forward, maximum backward. Collision geometry determines momentum transfer.

production_viz.label: delta_lambda vs theta: 0 at forward, 4.85pm at backscatter

beats.narration_text: Together with the photoelectric effect, the Compton effect made the case for photons unavoidable. Waves don't have elastic collisions. Particles do. Light is both — and in these experiments, the particle behavior is the only description that works.

production_viz.label: waves: no elastic collision; particles: yes. Light: both.

beats.narration_text: Delta-lambda equals h over m-e-c times 1 minus cosine-theta. At 90 degrees: 2.4 picometers, measured to 4 significant figures. Photon has momentum p equals h over lambda. Collision physics works. Classical wave theory predicts zero and is wrong.

card.copy: Δλ=(h/mₑc)(1−cosθ). At 90°: 2.4 pm measured. p=h/λ. Collision physics. Classical wave: wrong.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
